parts.content:
A wave on a string has a frequency of $25.0 \text{ Hz}$ and a wavelength of $0.80 \text{ m}$.

(i) Calculate the speed of the wave.

(ii) If the wave travels a distance of $120 \text{ m}$, calculate the time it takes.
parts.markscheme:
**(i)**

- State the formula for wave speed: $v = f \lambda$
- Substitute the values: $v = 25.0 \times 0.80$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the speed: $v = 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**(ii)**

- State the formula for time: $t = \frac{d}{v}$
- Substitute the values: $t = \frac{120}{20.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the time: $t = 6.0 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify wave properties
steps.content:
First, let's identify the variables given in the question for part (i). We are given the frequency ($f$) and the wavelength ($λ$).

*   $f = 25.0 \text{ Hz}$
*   $λ = 0.80 \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate wave speed
steps.content:
To find the speed ($v$), we use the wave speed formula found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet.

$$v = f\lambda$$

Now, substitute the values:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 25.0 \times 0.80 \\ v &= 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the frequency by the wavelength:

ti84.instructions: Multiply the frequency by the wavelength:

calculator.description: Calculate the wave speed

steps.title: Identify kinematic variables
steps.content:
For part (ii), we need to find the time ($t$) it takes for the wave to travel a specific distance ($d$). We will use the speed ($v$) we just calculated.

*   $d = 120 \text{ m}$
*   $v = 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate time taken
steps.content:
Since the wave travels at a constant speed, we can use the fundamental kinematic relationship:

$$t = \frac{d}{v}$$

Substitute the distance and speed:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{120}{20.0} \\ t &= 6.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This simple relationship connects to **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)**, where speed is defined as distance over time.

gdc.instructions: Divide the distance by the speed:

ti84.instructions: Divide the distance by the speed:

calculator.description: Calculate the time taken

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The final answers are:

(i) Speed: $$20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$
(ii) Time: $$6.0 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Identify wave speed formula
steps.content:
For part (i), we need to calculate the speed of the wave. The data booklet provides the relationship between wave speed ($v$), frequency ($f$), and wavelength ($\lambda$).

$$v = f\lambda$$

steps.title: Calculate wave speed
steps.content:
Substitute the given frequency ($25.0 \text{ Hz}$) and wavelength ($0.80 \text{ m}$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 25.0 \times 0.80 \\ v &= 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate wave speed

steps.title: Strategy: Period and Cycles
steps.content: For part (ii), we will use the **Period and Cycles Approach**. Instead of using speed directly, we can think of the time as the duration of one cycle (the period) multiplied by the number of cycles (wavelengths) that fit into the total distance.

steps.title: Calculate wave period
steps.content:
First, we determine the time it takes for one complete wave cycle, known as the period ($T$). The period is the reciprocal of the frequency ($f$):

$$T = \frac{1}{f}$$

Substituting the given frequency:

$$T = \frac{1}{25.0} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate period

steps.title: Find number of wavelengths
steps.content:
Next, we calculate how many complete wavelengths fit into the total distance the wave travels. This is found by dividing the total distance ($d$) by the wavelength ($\lambda$):

$$\text{Number of cycles} = \frac{d}{\lambda}$$

$$\text{Number of cycles} = \frac{120}{0.80} = 150$$

calculator.description: Calculate number of cycles

steps.title: Calculate total time
steps.content:
Finally, multiply the number of cycles by the time per cycle (the period) to find the total time:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= (\text{Number of cycles}) \times T \\ t &= 150 \times 0.04 \\ t &= 6.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total time

parts.content:
A sound wave in air has a speed of $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and a wavelength of $0.85 \text{ m}$.

(i) Calculate the frequency of the sound wave.

(ii) Calculate the time period of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
**(i)**

- State the rearranged formula for frequency: $f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$
- Substitute the values: $f = \frac{340}{0.85}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the frequency: $f = 400 \text{ Hz}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**(ii)**

- State the formula for time period: $T = \frac{1}{f}$
- Substitute the value: $T = \frac{1}{400}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the time period: $T = 0.0025 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content: First, we identify the values provided in the question for the sound wave. We are given the wave speed $v$ and the wavelength $\lambda$.

steps.title: Set up frequency equation
steps.content: To calculate the frequency in part (i), we use the wave speed formula from the data booklet. We rearrange this formula to solve for frequency $f$.

steps.title: Calculate frequency
steps.content:
Rearranging the formula gives $f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$. We substitute the known values to calculate the frequency.

$$f = \frac{340}{0.85} = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency

steps.title: Set up period equation
steps.content: For part (ii), we need to find the time period $T$. The time period is the reciprocal of the frequency. This is a fundamental definition covered in **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Calculate time period
steps.content:
Using the relationship $T = \frac{1}{f}$, we substitute the frequency we just calculated ($f = 400 \text{ Hz}$).

$$T = \frac{1}{400} = 0.0025 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time period

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the values provided in the question for the speed and wavelength of the sound wave:

*   Wave speed: $$v = 340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$
*   Wavelength: $$\lambda = 0.85 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Part (i): Select formula
steps.content: To find the frequency ($f$), we use the wave equation that relates wave speed, frequency, and wavelength. This is found in the **Waves** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Part (i): Calculate frequency
steps.content:
We rearrange the formula to solve for frequency ($f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$) and substitute the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{340}{0.85} \\ f &= 400 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate frequency

steps.title: Part (ii): Kinematics approach
steps.content:
To calculate the time period ($T$) safely, we will use the **Kinematics Approach**. Instead of relying on our calculated frequency from Part (i), we go back to the original data.

By definition, the period $T$ is the time it takes for the wave to travel a distance equal to one wavelength ($\lambda$). We can use the fundamental definition of constant speed:

$$ \text{Time} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} \implies T = \frac{\lambda}{v} $$

steps.title: Part (ii): Calculate period
steps.content:
Substituting the original given values into this equation allows us to find $T$ without carrying forward any potential errors from the frequency calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{0.85}{340} \\ T &= 0.0025 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate period using wavelength and speed

parts.content: Define a transverse wave and a longitudinal wave, and provide one example for each type.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** transverse wave: A wave in which the oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** example of a transverse wave: e.g., Light wave, water wave <marks>A1</marks>
- **Define** longitudinal wave: A wave in which the oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** example of a longitudinal wave: e.g., Sound wave, seismic P-wave <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify wave classification
steps.content: To answer this question, we need to classify waves based on the relationship between their **oscillation** (the movement of the medium or field) and the direction of **energy transfer** (the direction the wave travels). This fundamental concept is covered in **Topic C.2: Wave model**.

steps.title: Define transverse waves
steps.content:
A **transverse wave** is defined as a wave in which the oscillations are **perpendicular** (at $90^\circ$) to the direction of energy transfer. 

Think of a string being wiggled up and down while the wave itself moves horizontally.

steps.title: Example of transverse waves
steps.content: A standard physical example of a transverse wave is **light** (or any electromagnetic wave). Another common example is a **water wave** on the surface of a pond.

steps.title: Define longitudinal waves
steps.content:
A **longitudinal wave** is defined as a wave in which the oscillations are **parallel** to the direction of energy transfer.

In these waves, the medium moves back and forth in the same direction that the wave is travelling, creating regions of compression and rarefaction.

steps.title: Example of longitudinal waves
steps.content: The most common physical example of a longitudinal wave is a **sound wave**. Another example is a **seismic P-wave** (primary wave) during an earthquake.

steps.title: Final Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 4 marks, your response should look like this:

1.  **Transverse Wave Definition:** Oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer. (1 mark)
2.  **Transverse Example:** Light waves. (1 mark)
3.  **Longitudinal Wave Definition:** Oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer. (1 mark)
4.  **Longitudinal Example:** Sound waves. (1 mark)

parts.content: A sound wave has a frequency of 440 Hz and a wavelength of 0.77 m. Calculate the speed of the wave, and explain why sound waves are classified as longitudinal waves.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** values into the wave speed formula ($v = f\lambda$): $v = 440 \times 0.77$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the speed: $v = 338.8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (accept $339 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** classification: Sound waves are longitudinal because the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain two differences between mechanical waves and electromagnetic waves. Provide one example of each type of wave.
parts.markscheme:
- **Distinguish** by medium: Mechanical waves require a medium for propagation (e.g., air, water, or a solid), whereas electromagnetic waves do not require a medium and can travel through a vacuum <marks>A1</marks>
- **Distinguish** by oscillation: Mechanical waves involve the oscillation of particles in the medium, whereas electromagnetic waves involve oscillations of electric and magnetic fields <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** example of a mechanical wave: e.g., Sound wave, water wave <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** example of an electromagnetic wave: e.g., Light wave, X-ray <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Categorizing Wave Types
steps.content: To understand the differences between mechanical and electromagnetic waves, we look at Topic **C.2 Wave model**. In physics, waves are disturbances that transfer energy from one point to another. These disturbances can be categorized based on how they travel (propagation) and what exactly is moving back and forth (oscillation).

steps.title: Propagation and the Medium
steps.content:
The first major difference lies in the **propagation medium**. 

*   **Mechanical waves** require a physical medium (matter) to travel through. They cannot exist in a vacuum because they rely on the interaction of atoms or molecules.
*   **Electromagnetic (EM) waves** do not require a medium. They can propagate through the vacuum of space because they consist of self-sustaining fields.

This earns you the first mark by distinguishing the requirement for a physical medium versus a vacuum.

highlights.text: Mechanical waves require a medium for propagation (e.g., air, water, or a solid), whereas electromagnetic waves do not require a medium and can travel through a vacuum

steps.title: Nature of the Oscillation
steps.content:
The second difference is the **nature of the oscillation**—the "stuff" that is actually waving.

*   **Mechanical waves** involve the oscillation of **matter particles** (like air molecules or water droplets).
*   **Electromagnetic waves** involve the oscillation of **electric and magnetic fields** that are perpendicular to each other and the direction of energy transfer.

Identifying this difference in what oscillates provides the second mark.

highlights.text: Mechanical waves involve the oscillation of particles in the medium, whereas electromagnetic waves involve oscillations of electric and magnetic fields

steps.title: Providing Specific Examples
steps.content:
Finally, we need to provide one clear example for each type to secure the final two marks:

1.  **Mechanical Wave Example:** Sound waves (which oscillate air particles) or seismic waves (which oscillate the ground).
2.  **Electromagnetic Wave Example:** Visible light, X-rays, or Radio waves (all of which consist of oscillating $E$ and $B$ fields).

**Final Answer Summary:**
*   Difference 1: Medium vs. Vacuum.
*   Difference 2: Particle oscillation vs. Field oscillation.
*   Mechanical Example: Sound waves.
*   EM Example: Light waves.

highlights.text: State example of a mechanical wave: e.g., Sound wave, water wave
State example of an electromagnetic wave: e.g., Light wave, X-ray

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks for two differences between mechanical and electromagnetic (EM) waves, specifically focusing on how they are classified geometrically (transverse vs. longitudinal) and how fast they travel. We also need to provide one example for each type to secure all 4 marks.

steps.title: Compare wave geometry
steps.content:
One major difference is their **wave geometry** (how the oscillations are oriented relative to the direction of energy transfer):

*   **Mechanical waves** can be either **transverse** (oscillations are perpendicular to propagation) or **longitudinal** (oscillations are parallel to propagation).
*   **Electromagnetic waves** are strictly **transverse**. They consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that are always perpendicular to the direction of the wave's travel.

This classification is covered in **Topic C.2 Wave model**.

steps.title: Compare propagation speeds
steps.content:
A second difference is their **propagation speed**:

*   **Electromagnetic waves** travel at a universal constant speed in a vacuum, known as the speed of light:
    $$c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$$
*   **Mechanical waves** travel much slower because they depend on the physical displacement of particles in a medium. For example, sound (a mechanical wave) travels at approximately $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in air.

The speed of light is significantly higher than any mechanical wave speed.

steps.content:
To complete the answer, we provide specific examples for each category:

*   **Mechanical wave example:** Sound waves or water waves.
*   **Electromagnetic wave example:** Visible light, X-rays, or Radio waves.

In the exam, stating one example for each is worth 1 mark per example (A1 + A1).

parts.content:
A light wave has a frequency of $6.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$ and travels in air with a speed of $3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Calculate the wavelength of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
- State the wave equation: $v = f \lambda$
- Substitute the given values into the equation: $\lambda = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{6.0 \times 10^{14}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the wavelength: $\lambda = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$ (or $500 \text{ nm}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: To solve this problem, we first need to extract the values provided in the question. We are given the frequency and the speed of the light wave.

steps.title: Select the wave equation
steps.content: In **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we learn that the speed of a wave is the product of its frequency and wavelength. You can find this formula in the **Waves** section of your IB Physics data booklet.

steps.title: Rearrange for wavelength
steps.content:
Since we need to calculate the wavelength ($\lambda$), we rearrange the formula by dividing both sides by frequency ($f$):

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the rearranged equation to find the wavelength. This step earns the first mark ($M1$) in the markscheme.

$$\lambda = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{6.0 \times 10^{14}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division using the scientific notation format: (3.0E8) / (6.0E14).

ti84.instructions: Use the [EE] key (2nd + ,) for scientific notation. Enter (3.0 E 8) / (6.0 E 14).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength using scientific notation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives us the final wavelength. Don't forget to include the correct units (meters). 

$$\lambda = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

Note: This is equivalent to $500 \text{ nm}$, which is in the visible spectrum (green/yellow light). This final result earns the second mark ($A1$).

parts.content: State what is meant by the term wavelength.
parts.markscheme:
- State the definition of wavelength: The distance between two consecutive points in phase on a wave, such as crest to crest or trough to trough <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the property
steps.content: In this question, we are asked to define **wavelength**. This is a fundamental property of a wave that describes its spatial extent. In **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we learn that waves repeat themselves in both time (period) and space (wavelength).

steps.title: Define using spatial phase
steps.content:
To define wavelength using the **Spatial Phase** approach, we focus on the distance between points that are doing the exact same thing at the same time. We call these points "in phase."

Formally, wavelength is the distance between two consecutive points on a wave that are **in phase** with each other.

steps.title: Apply to wave features
steps.content:
In a ripple tank, it is easiest to measure this distance between the highest points (crests) or the lowest points (troughs). 

Therefore, the complete definition for your exam is:
**The distance between two consecutive points in phase on a wave, such as from one crest to the next crest.**

highlights.text: The distance between two consecutive points in phase on a wave, such as crest to crest or trough to trough

steps.title: Relate to wave equation
steps.content:
While not required for the definition itself, you can always find wavelength ($\lambda$) if you know the wave speed ($v$) and frequency ($f$) using the formula from your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content: In this question, we need to define **wavelength**. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**. While many students think of it simply as the distance between crests, we can also define it by looking at the timing of the wave source.

steps.title: Connect oscillation and motion
steps.content:
Think about the source of the wave (like the dipper in a ripple tank). It takes a specific amount of time to complete one full up-and-down movement, which we call the **period** ($T$). 

During this single oscillation, the wave energy and the wavefront move forward through the medium. The distance that the wave travels during exactly one period is the wavelength ($\lambda$).

steps.title: Use the wave equation
steps.content:
We can see this relationship clearly using the wave equation from your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Since frequency ($f$) is the inverse of the period ($T$), where $f = \frac{1}{T}$, we can rearrange the formula to show that the distance traveled ($\lambda$) is the speed multiplied by the time for one cycle:

$$\lambda = v \times T$$

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Based on the wave cycle approach, the formal definition is:

**Wavelength is the distance traveled by a wavefront (or the wave energy) during one complete oscillation or period of the source.**

parts.content:
The students observe that 8 crests pass a point in $4.0$ seconds.

Calculate the frequency of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the formula for frequency: $f = \dfrac{\text{number of crests}}{\text{time}}$
- Substitute values: $f = \dfrac{8}{4.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer: $f = 2.0 \text{ Hz}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content:
The distance between five consecutive crests is measured to be $20.0 \text{ cm}$.

Determine the wavelength of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the number of wavelengths: Distance between 5 consecutive crests corresponds to 4 wavelengths
- Calculate wavelength: $\lambda = \dfrac{20.0 \text{ cm}}{4}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Convert to metres: $\lambda = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the wave pattern
steps.content: To find the wavelength ($\lambda$), we first need to understand the relationship between the number of crests and the number of full wave cycles. In Topic **C.2 Wave model**, we learn that one wavelength is the distance between two consecutive crests.

steps.title: Determine wavelength count
steps.content:
Using the **Analytical Counting** method, we recognize that the number of full wavelengths is always one less than the number of consecutive crests ($n - 1$).

Since there are 5 crests, the number of wavelengths is:
$$5 - 1 = 4 \text{ wavelengths}$$

steps.title: Calculate numerical value
steps.content:
Now, we divide the total measured distance by the number of wavelengths to find the length of a single wave:

$$\lambda = \frac{20.0 \text{ cm}}{4} = 5.0 \text{ cm}$$

This calculation earns the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

ti84.instructions: Enter 20.0 divided by 4 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide the total distance by the number of wave cycles.

steps.title: Convert to standard units
steps.content:
In IB Physics, it is standard practice to provide final answers in SI units (metres). We convert centimeters to metres by dividing by $100$:

$$\lambda = \frac{5.0}{100} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

Stating the final answer clearly with units earns the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Divide 5.0 by 100 on the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5.0 divided by 100 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert centimeters to metres.

steps.title: Visualize the wave pattern
steps.content:
To find the wavelength, we first need to determine how many full waves fit into the measured distance. It's helpful to sketch out the crests to avoid a common mistake: thinking that 5 crests mean 5 wavelengths.

Let's represent each crest with a vertical line:

$$| \xrightarrow{\lambda} | \xrightarrow{\lambda} | \xrightarrow{\lambda} | \xrightarrow{\lambda} |$$
$$C_1 \quad \quad C_2 \quad \quad C_3 \quad \quad C_4 \quad \quad C_5$$

By sketching this, we can clearly see that there are only **4 spaces** (wavelengths) between the 1st and the 5th crest.

steps.title: Set up the calculation
steps.content:
Now that we know there are 4 wavelengths $(\lambda)$ in the total distance of $20.0 \text{ cm}$, we can set up our equation:

$$4\lambda = 20.0 \text{ cm}$$

To find one wavelength, we divide the total distance by the number of intervals:

$$\lambda = \frac{20.0 \text{ cm}}{4}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 20.0 divided by 4 on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength in centimeters.

steps.title: Calculate and convert units
steps.content:
Performing the division gives us the wavelength in centimeters:

$$\lambda = 5.0 \text{ cm}$$

Since IB Physics problems usually require SI units (meters), we convert this by dividing by $100$:

$$\lambda = \frac{5.0}{100} \text{ m} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

This matches the requirement for 2 marks: identifying the 4 intervals and providing the correct final value with units.

calculator.description: Convert centimeters to meters.

parts.content: Using your answers from parts 2 and 3, determine the speed of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the wave speed formula: $v = f \lambda$
- Substitute values: $v = 2.0 \times 0.050$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer with units: $v = 0.10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
To calculate the speed of the wave, we need the frequency ($f$) and the wavelength ($\lambda$) obtained from the previous parts of the question:

*   From part 2, the frequency is $f = 2.0 \text{ Hz}$.
*   From part 3, the wavelength is $\lambda = 0.050 \text{ m}$.

This problem falls under **Topic C.2: Wave model**, which describes the fundamental relationship between these properties.

steps.title: Select the wave equation
steps.content: In the IB Physics Data Booklet, under the **Waves** section, we find the formula that relates wave speed, frequency, and wavelength.

steps.title: Calculate wave speed
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the equation to find the speed ($v$):

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f \times \lambda \\ v &= 2.0 \times 0.050 \end{aligned}$$

Performing this calculation gives us the speed in meters per second.

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation screen, type 2.0 * 0.050 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the wave speed by multiplying frequency and wavelength.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The speed of the wave is:

$$v = 0.10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
*   **M1**: For correctly substituting the values from parts 2 and 3 into the wave speed formula.
*   **A1**: For the correct final value of $0.10$ with appropriate units ($\text{m s}^{-1}$).

steps.title: Identify the wave type
steps.content: In physics, waves are categorized based on how the particles in the medium move relative to the direction the wave is traveling. This concept is covered in **Topic C.2: Wave model**. We are specifically looking for the definition of a **transverse wave**.

highlights.text: State what is meant by a transverse wave.

steps.title: Analyze particle motion
steps.content: Think about the ducks in the diagram. As the wave travels to the right, the ducks (and the water particles they sit on) don't move to the right with the wave; instead, they move **up and down**. This up-and-down movement is called **oscillation** or vibration.

steps.title: State the formal definition
steps.content:
To provide the formal definition required for the mark, we relate that oscillation to the direction of energy transfer. 

A **transverse wave** is a wave in which the direction of particle oscillation (or vibration) is **perpendicular** (at $90^\circ$) to the direction of energy transfer (or wave travel).

In this case, the energy moves right, while the particles oscillate vertically.

parts.content: The frequency of the wave is $0.62\text{ Hz}$. Calculate the speed of the wave.
parts.markscheme: $v = f\lambda = 0.62 \times 16 = 9.9\text{ m}\text{s}^{-1}$ «or $9.92\text{ m}\text{s}^{-1}$» <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To calculate the wave speed, we first need to identify the frequency ($f$) provided in the question. 

This question relates to **Topic C.2: Wave model**, where we study the fundamental properties and relationships of waves.

highlights.text: The frequency of the wave is 0.62 Hz

steps.title: Determine the wavelength
steps.content:
Next, we need to find the wavelength ($\lambda$) from the provided graph. The wavelength is the horizontal distance between two consecutive identical points on the wave, such as two peaks or two troughs.

Looking at the graph:
- A peak occurs at $x = 4\text{ m}$.
- The next peak occurs at $x = 20\text{ m}$.

$$\lambda = 20\text{ m} - 4\text{ m} = 16\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Apply the wave equation
steps.content:
Now we can use the wave equation from the data booklet to calculate the speed ($v$). 

Substitute our values:
- $f = 0.62\text{ Hz}$
- $\lambda = 16\text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ v &= 0.62 \times 16 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.62 * 16 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the wave speed using the product of frequency and wavelength.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation, we find:

$$v = 9.92\text{ m}\text{s}^{-1}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as given in the frequency), we get $9.9\text{ m}\text{s}^{-1}$. Both answers are typically accepted in the IB markscheme.

parts.content: Show that the phase difference between the oscillations of the two ducks is $\pi$ radians.
parts.markscheme:
## Method 1
Phase difference is $\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times \text{separation} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{2}$ or $\frac{2\pi}{16} \times 8$

$$ = \pi $$ <marks>A1</marks>

## Method 2
One wavelength/period represents a phase difference of $2\pi$ and the ducks are $\frac{1}{2}$ wavelength/period apart, so the phase difference is $\pi$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Determine the wavelength
steps.content:
To find the phase difference, we first need to identify the wavelength ($\lambda$) from the graph. The wavelength is the horizontal distance between two consecutive identical points, such as two peaks (crests).

Looking at the graph, there is a peak at $x = 0\text{ cm}$ and the next peak is at $x = 16\text{ cm}$. Therefore:
$$\lambda = 16\text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Find the horizontal separation
steps.content:
Next, we need to find the horizontal distance ($\Delta x$) between the two ducks, A and B.

From the graph:
- Duck A is at $x = 4\text{ cm}$
- Duck B is at $x = 12\text{ cm}$

The horizontal separation is:
$$\Delta x = 12 - 4 = 8\text{ cm}$$

highlights.text: A and B show two ducks floating on the water surface

steps.title: Apply phase difference formula
steps.content:
We can now use the phase difference formula which relates the physical separation of two points to the wave's cycle. A full wavelength corresponds to a phase difference of $2\pi$ radians.

The formula is:
$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \Delta x}{\lambda}$$

Substitute the values we found:
$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \times 8}{16}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Simplify the fraction to find the phase difference:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{16\pi}{16} = \pi\text{ radians}$$

This confirms that the ducks are exactly half a wavelength apart, resulting in an out-of-phase oscillation of $\pi$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression (2 * π * 8) / 16 in the run-matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * π * 8) / 16 and press ENTER. Then press π and ENTER to compare.

desmos.instructions: Type (2 * pi * 8) / 16 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the phase difference numerically to verify the result is pi.

highlights.text: phase difference between the oscillations of the two ducks is $\pi$ radians

steps.title: Analyze the wave diagram
steps.content:
To find the phase difference, we first look at the positions of the two ducks on the wave. 

- Duck **A** is located at a **crest** (the highest point).
- Duck **B** is located at the very next **trough** (the lowest point).

In a standard transverse wave, the distance from one crest to the next is one full wavelength ($\lambda$). Therefore, the distance between a crest and the immediate next trough is exactly half of a wavelength.

highlights.text: A and B show two ducks floating on the water surface

steps.title: Determine wavelength fraction
steps.content:
The **Wavelength Proportion Approach** relies on identifying how much of a full cycle exists between two points. Since the distance from duck $A$ to duck $B$ is half a wavelength, we can express this separation as a fraction:

$$\text{Fraction of cycle} = \frac{\text{Separation}}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate phase difference
steps.content:
A full wave cycle ($1\lambda$) corresponds to a phase difference of $2\pi$ radians. To find the phase difference ($\phi$) for the two ducks, we multiply the fraction of the cycle by $2\pi$:

$$\phi = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi$$

$$\phi = \pi \text{ radians}$$

This shows that the two ducks are exactly out of phase (specifically, $180^\circ$ or $\pi$ radians apart).

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of pi by multiplying the fraction (1/2) by the full cycle constant (2).

highlights.text: phase difference between the oscillations of the two ducks is π radians

steps.title: Analyze the duck positions
steps.content:
To determine the phase difference, we first look at the vertical positions of the ducks on the wave profile in the diagram. 

Looking at the graph:
- **Duck A** is located at a **crest** (the maximum positive displacement).
- **Duck B** is located at a **trough** (the maximum negative displacement).

highlights.text: A and B show two ducks floating on the water surface

steps.title: Identify the antiphase relationship
steps.content:
In wave physics, when one point is at a crest and another is at a trough simultaneously, they are described as being in **antiphase** (or completely out of phase). 

This means that as duck A moves down toward the equilibrium position, duck B will be moving up toward the equilibrium position. Their oscillations are perfectly opposite.

steps.title: Convert to radians
steps.content:
We know from **Topic C.2 (Wave model)** that a full wave cycle (one wavelength) corresponds to a phase difference of $2\pi$ radians.

Since antiphase represents exactly half of a full cycle ($180^\circ$), we calculate the phase difference as:

$$\text{Phase Difference} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi = \pi \text{ radians}$$

parts.content: State the two conditions required for the motion of the mass to be defined as simple harmonic.
parts.markscheme:
*   The acceleration of the mass is directly proportional to its displacement from the equilibrium position. <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   The acceleration is always directed towards the equilibrium position (or opposite to the direction of displacement). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
In a preliminary test, the engineer observes that adding a weight of $2.80 \mathrm{\,N}$ to the spring results in a static extension of $0.070 \mathrm{\,m}$.

Calculate the spring constant $k$ of the spring.
parts.markscheme: $k = \frac{F}{x} = \frac{2.80}{0.070} = 40 \mathrm{\,N\,m^{-1}}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the period of oscillation $T$ when the $0.350 \mathrm{\,kg}$ mass is attached to this spring.
parts.markscheme:
Period formula: $T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{0.350}{40}} = 0.588 \mathrm{\,s}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the frequency of these oscillations.
parts.markscheme: $$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.588} = 1.70 \mathrm{\,Hz}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The mass is pulled down by an amplitude of $4.50 \mathrm{\,cm}$ and then released. Calculate the angular frequency $\omega$ of the motion.
parts.markscheme:
Angular frequency: $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.350}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$\omega = 10.7 \mathrm{\,rad\,s^{-1}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the maximum acceleration of the mass during the cycle.
parts.markscheme:
Maximum acceleration: $a_{max} = \omega^2 A$ 

$$a_{max} = (10.69)^2 \times 0.045 = 5.14 \mathrm{\,m\,s^{-2}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student investigates how the wavelength $\lambda$ of sound in air depends on frequency $f$. To analyze the data, the student plots a graph of $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$. Each data point includes an uncertainty in wavelength of $\pm 0.01 \mathrm{~m}$. The graph is shown below.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/2aeca970-2fac-48b6-89f8-9240b9a7bf94.png)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.2 Wave model > C.2.2 Nature of wave propagation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2 Nature of wave propagation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the wave equation v = fλ and its rearrangement λ = v(1/f) to interpret a linear plot of wavelength vs inverse frequency and estimate the speed of sound from the gradient.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between wavelength and frequency as shown by this graph.
parts.markscheme: Wavelength $\lambda$ is directly proportional to $\frac{1}{f}$ because the graph is a straight line that passes through the origin. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph axes
steps.content:
First, we look at what is being plotted. The vertical axis represents the wavelength $\lambda$ and the horizontal axis represents the reciprocal of frequency $\frac{1}{f}$.

This is a common technique in physics to linearize data that might otherwise look like a curve.

highlights.text: plots a graph of λ against 1/f

steps.title: Identify the graph shape
steps.content:
Looking at the provided plot, we can see that the line of best fit is a **straight line** that passes through the **origin** $(0,0)$. 

In IB Physics, these two characteristics (linearity and passing through the origin) are the defining features of a direct proportionality between the variables on the axes.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Because the graph is a straight line through the origin, we conclude that the variable on the y-axis ($\lambda$) is **directly proportional** to the variable on the x-axis ($\frac{1}{f}$).

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{f}$$

This relationship is consistent with the wave equation from your data booklet, $$v = f\lambda$$, which can be rearranged to $$\lambda = v \cdot \left(\frac{1}{f}\right)$$, where the wave speed $v$ is the constant gradient of the line.

steps.title: Analyze the graph axes
steps.content:
To understand the relationship, we first look at what variables are being compared. The graph shows wavelength $\lambda$ on the vertical y-axis and the reciprocal of frequency $\frac{1}{f}$ on the horizontal x-axis.

This setup is common in **Topic C.2 (Wave model)** when we want to test the wave equation relationship:

$$v = f\lambda$$

steps.title: Observe the linear trend
steps.content:
Notice the shape of the graph. The data points form a clear **straight line** that passes through the **origin** $(0,0)$. 

In physics and math, when a graph of $y$ versus $x$ is a straight line through the origin, we say that $y$ is **directly proportional** to $x$. In this specific case:

$$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{f}$$

steps.title: Identify the inverse relationship
steps.content:
While $\lambda$ is directly proportional to the *inverse* of frequency ($\frac{1}{f}$), this means that $\lambda$ has an **inverse relationship** with frequency ($f$) itself. 

As the frequency increases, the wavelength must decrease proportionally to keep the wave speed constant. This is exactly what an inverse proportion looks like mathematically.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the evidence that the graph of $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$ is a straight line through the origin, we conclude:

**Wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency.**

(Note: The markscheme specifically looks for the observation that the graph is a straight line through the origin to award the mark).

highlights.text: Wavelength $\lambda$ is directly proportional to $\frac{1}{f}$ because the graph is a straight line that passes through the origin.

parts.content: Explain why the student plotted $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$, rather than against $f$.
parts.markscheme:
From the wave equation $v = f \lambda$, we have $\lambda = v \left(\frac{1}{f}\right)$. <marks>1</marks>
Plotting $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$ linearizes the relationship, where the gradient of the straight line represents the speed of sound $v$. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Start with wave equation
steps.content:
To understand why we choose specific axes for a graph, we always start with the fundamental physics equation that relates our variables. For sound waves, we use the wave equation found in your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

In this experiment, the student is measuring how wavelength ($\lambda$) changes as frequency ($f$) varies.

steps.title: Rearrange for the y-axis
steps.content:
The student chose to plot $\lambda$ on the vertical ($y$) axis. To see the relationship clearly, we should rearrange the equation to solve for $\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ \lambda &= \frac{v}{f} \end{aligned}$$

This can be written as:

$$\lambda = v \left( \frac{1}{f} \right)$$

This is a crucial step for earning your first mark!

steps.title: Compare to linear form
steps.content:
Now, we compare our rearranged equation to the general equation for a straight line through the origin, $y = mx$:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= m \cdot x \\ \lambda &= v \cdot \left( \frac{1}{f} \right) \end{aligned}$$

By plotting $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$, the relationship becomes **linear**. If the student had plotted $\lambda$ against $f$, the graph would have been a curve (a hyperbola), which is much harder to analyze accurately.

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.content:
The main advantage of this "linearization" is that the **gradient** of the resulting straight line represents a physical constant. 

In this case, the gradient $m$ is equal to the speed of sound $v$. It is much easier for a student to calculate a constant slope from a straight line than to interpret a curve.

steps.title: Identify the wave relationship
steps.content:
To understand why we choose specific axes for a graph, we first look at the physics equation that relates our variables. In **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we find the wave equation:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Where:
- $v$ is the speed of sound (constant in a specific medium like air)
- $f$ is the frequency
- $\lambda$ is the wavelength

If we rearrange this to solve for wavelength, we get:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

steps.title: Analyze the curve (λ vs f)
steps.content:
If the student plotted $\lambda$ against $f$, the relationship $\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$ shows that wavelength is **inversely proportional** to frequency. 

Mathematically, this takes the form $y = \frac{k}{x}$, which produces a **hyperbola** (a curve). Curves are difficult to analyze in a lab setting because:
1. It is hard to draw an accurate "curve of best fit" by eye.
2. The gradient of a curve changes at every point, making it difficult to determine a single value for the speed of sound.

steps.title: Analyze the line (λ vs 1/f)
steps.content:
By plotting $\lambda$ against $\frac{1}{f}$, the student **linearizes** the data. We can rewrite the equation in the form of a straight line, $y = mx + c$:

$$\lambda = v \left( \frac{1}{f} \right) + 0$$

In this setup:
- $y$ is $\lambda$
- $x$ is $\frac{1}{f}$
- The gradient ($m$) is the **speed of sound ($v$)**
- The $y$-intercept ($c$) should be zero.

A straight line is much more useful because we can easily draw a line of best fit using a ruler and identify **systematic errors** if the line does not pass through the origin.

steps.title: Visualize with a calculator
steps.content: You can use your GDC to see how changing the x-axis variable transforms a curve into a straight line. If we assume the speed of sound is roughly $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, let's compare the two functions.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Graph menu.
2. Graph f1(x) = 340/x.
3. Graph f2(x) = 340x.
4. Observe that the first is a curve and the second is a straight line through the origin.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 340/X (represents λ vs f).
3. Enter Y2 = 340*X (represents λ vs 1/f, where X is the value of 1/f).
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 340/x to see the hyperbola.
2. Type y = 340x to see the linear relationship used in the student's graph.

calculator.description: Graphing the inverse relationship vs the linear relationship.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content: In summary, the student uses $\frac{1}{f}$ to produce a **straight-line graph**. This allows for a simple calculation of the speed of sound from the **gradient** and makes it easier to account for uncertainties and potential systematic errors in the experiment.

parts.content: Use the graph to estimate the speed of sound in air.
parts.markscheme:
The speed of sound is the gradient of the line.
Choose two points from the line of best fit, e.g., $(0.0005, 0.17)$ and $(0.0025, 0.85)$. <marks>M1</marks>
$\text{Gradient} = \frac{0.85 - 0.17}{0.0025 - 0.0005} = \frac{0.68}{0.0020} = 340 \mathrm{~m/s}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Link graph to physics
steps.content:
To find the speed of sound, we first need to look at the relationship between wavelength ($\lambda$) and frequency ($f$). From the **Waves** section of your data booklet, we have the wave equation:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Since the graph plots $\lambda$ on the $y$-axis and $\frac{1}{f}$ on the $x$-axis, we can rearrange the formula to match the linear form $y = mx$:

$$\lambda = v \left( \frac{1}{f} \right)$$

By comparing this to $y = mx$, we can see that the **gradient ($m$) of the line is equal to the speed of sound ($v$)**.

steps.title: Select coordinates from line
steps.content:
To calculate the gradient, we pick two points directly from the line of best fit. It's best practice in IB Physics to choose points that are far apart to reduce the impact of reading errors.

Looking at the graph, two clear points on the line are:
- Point 1: $(x_1, y_1) = (0.0005, 0.17)$
- Point 2: $(x_2, y_2) = (0.0025, 0.85)$

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Now, we apply the gradient formula:

$$\text{Gradient} (m) = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

Substituting our chosen values:

$$\text{Gradient} = \frac{0.85 - 0.17}{0.0025 - 0.0005} = \frac{0.68}{0.0020}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference in y-values and x-values, then divide.

desmos.instructions: Enter the gradient calculation directly into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope using the change in y divided by the change in x.

steps.title: State final speed
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us:

$$\text{Gradient} = 340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

Since the gradient is equal to the speed, the estimated speed of sound in air is **$340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$**. 

In an IB exam, one mark is typically awarded for correctly identifying two points on the line and showing the gradient calculation, and the second mark is for the final answer with correct units.

steps.title: Link Equation to Graph
steps.content:
To find the speed of sound, we first need to relate the variables on the graph to a physical law. In **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we learn the wave equation. 

The relationship between wave speed ($v$), frequency ($f$), and wavelength ($\lambda$) is:

$$v = f \lambda$$

The graph plots $\lambda$ on the $y$-axis and $\frac{1}{f}$ on the $x$-axis. We can rearrange the equation to match the linear form $y = mx$:

$$\lambda = v \times \left(\frac{1}{f}\right)$$

This shows that the speed of sound ($v$) is the gradient of our line.

steps.title: Select a Representative Point
steps.content:
Since the line of best fit passes through the origin $(0,0)$, we can estimate the speed of sound by choosing a single representative point $(x, y)$ from the line and calculating the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$.

Looking at the line of best fit, a clear point to read is at the far right to ensure better precision:
- $x$-coordinate ($\frac{1}{f}$) $= 0.0025 \text{ s}$
- $y$-coordinate ($\lambda$) $= 0.85 \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate the Speed
steps.content:
Now, substitute these values into the rearranged wave equation to solve for $v$:

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{1/f}$$

$$v = \frac{0.85}{0.0025}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.85 divided by 0.0025 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the speed of sound by dividing wavelength by the inverse of frequency.

steps.title: State Final Result
steps.content:
The calculation gives us a speed of $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. This value is consistent with the standard speed of sound in air.

Final Answer:
$$v = 340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

parts.content: Use the uncertainty in the graph ($\pm 0.01 \mathrm{~m}$) to estimate the uncertainty in the gradient. State your final result for the speed of sound with appropriate uncertainty.
parts.markscheme:
Determine the maximum or minimum gradient using the error bars.
Maximum gradient using the points $(0.0005, 0.17 - 0.01)$ and $(0.0025, 0.85 + 0.01)$:
$m_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.86 - 0.16}{0.0020} = 350 \mathrm{~m/s}$.
Minimum gradient using the points $(0.0005, 0.17 + 0.01)$ and $(0.0025, 0.85 - 0.01)$:
$m_{\text{min}} = \frac{0.84 - 0.18}{0.0020} = 330 \mathrm{~m/s}$. <marks>M1</marks>
$\text{Uncertainty} = \frac{350 - 330}{2} = 10 \mathrm{~m/s}$. <marks>A1</marks>
Final result: $v = 340 \pm 10 \mathrm{~m/s}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Link gradient to physics
steps.content:
First, we identify that the gradient of a $\lambda$ vs $\frac{1}{f}$ graph represents the speed of sound. From the wave equation in the **Waves** section of the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging to match the linear form $y = mx$, we get:

$$\lambda = v \left( \frac{1}{f} \right)$$

Here, $\lambda$ is the $y$-variable, $\frac{1}{f}$ is the $x$-variable, and the gradient $m$ is equal to the speed of sound $v$.

steps.title: Calculate Maximum Gradient
steps.content:
To find the maximum possible gradient ($m_{\text{max}}$), we draw a line that passes from the bottom of the error bar of the first point to the top of the error bar of the last point.

Using the extreme points $(0.0005, 0.17)$ and $(0.0025, 0.85)$ and the given uncertainty:
- Point 1 (bottom): $y_1 = 0.17 - 0.01 = 0.16$
- Point 2 (top): $y_2 = 0.85 + 0.01 = 0.86$

$$m_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.86 - 0.16}{0.0025 - 0.0005} = 350 \text{ m/s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate maximum gradient

steps.title: Calculate Minimum Gradient
steps.content:
To find the minimum possible gradient ($m_{\text{min}}$), we draw a line from the top of the first error bar to the bottom of the last error bar:
- Point 1 (top): $y_1 = 0.17 + 0.01 = 0.18$
- Point 2 (bottom): $y_2 = 0.85 - 0.01 = 0.84$

$$m_{\text{min}} = \frac{0.84 - 0.18}{0.0025 - 0.0005} = 330 \text{ m/s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate minimum gradient

steps.title: Determine uncertainty and speed
steps.content:
The uncertainty in the gradient (speed) is calculated as half the difference between the max and min gradients:

$$\Delta v = \frac{m_{\text{max}} - m_{\text{min}}}{2} = \frac{350 - 330}{2} = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

The best estimate for the speed is the average of the two gradients:

$$v = \frac{350 + 330}{2} = 340 \text{ m/s}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the range and divide by 2: (350 - 330) / 2.

calculator.description: Calculate uncertainty

steps.title: State final result
steps.content:
Combining our best estimate and the calculated uncertainty, we state the final result for the speed of sound with the correct units and appropriate significant figures.

$$v = 340 \pm 10 \text{ m/s}$$

steps.title: Relate gradient to speed
steps.content:
To find the speed of sound, we first look at the relationship between wavelength $\lambda$ and frequency $f$. Using the wave equation from the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging for $\lambda$ to match the form of the graph ($y = mx + c$):

$$\lambda = v \left( \frac{1}{f} \right)$$

Since $\lambda$ is on the $y$-axis and $\frac{1}{f}$ is on the $x$-axis, the gradient ($m$) of the line represents the speed of sound $v$.

steps.title: Calculate best-fit gradient
steps.content:
Using the line of best fit from the graph, we select two points to calculate the gradient. Let's use the first point $(0.0005, 0.17)$ and the last point $(0.0025, 0.85)$.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{best} &= \frac{\Delta \lambda}{\Delta (1/f)} \\ m_{best} &= \frac{0.85 - 0.17}{0.0025 - 0.0005} \\ m_{best} &= \frac{0.68}{0.0020} \\ m_{best} &= 340 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the change in y (0.68) by the change in x (0.002).

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of the best-fit line.

steps.title: Calculate maximum gradient
steps.content:
To find the maximum possible gradient ($m_{max}$), we use the uncertainty in $\lambda$. We draw the steepest possible line that passes through the error bars. This line goes from the bottom of the first error bar to the top of the last error bar.

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_{start, min} &= 0.17 - 0.01 = 0.16 \text{ m} \\ \lambda_{end, max} &= 0.85 + 0.01 = 0.86 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Now calculate $m_{max}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{max} &= \frac{0.86 - 0.16}{0.0025 - 0.0005} \\ m_{max} &= \frac{0.70}{0.0020} \\ m_{max} &= 350 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the steep gradient using the top of the last point and bottom of the first.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum possible gradient using error bars.

steps.title: Estimate uncertainty and result
steps.content:
The uncertainty in the gradient is the absolute difference between the best-fit gradient and the extreme gradient:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Uncertainty} &= |m_{max} - m_{best}| \\ &= |350 - 340| \\ &= 10 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Finally, we state the speed of sound with its uncertainty, ensuring they both have the same precision (the uncertainty is usually quoted to 1 significant figure).

$$v = 340 \pm 10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

parts.content: Suggest two improvements to the experimental method or data collection that could reduce the uncertainty in this investigation.
parts.markscheme:
Collect more data points over a wider range of frequencies to improve the reliability of the best-fit line. <marks>1</marks>
Use a more precise method for measuring wavelength, such as using an oscilloscope or computer-assisted sound analysis to reduce reading error. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the experimental reliability
steps.content: To reduce uncertainty in a graphical analysis, we need to focus on how the line of best fit is determined. Currently, the graph contains a limited number of data points over a narrow range of $\frac{1}{f}$. This makes the line sensitive to small errors in individual measurements.

steps.title: Increase the data set
steps.content:
The first improvement is to **collect more data points**. By measuring the wavelength at a larger number of different frequencies, we increase the size of our data set. 

In statistical terms, a larger data set reduces the impact of random errors on the gradient. If one point is slightly off, its influence on the overall line of best fit is minimized when there are many other points to anchor the line.

steps.title: Extend the frequency range
steps.content:
The second improvement is to **extend the range of frequencies** used. Currently, the data only covers a specific portion of the $\frac{1}{f}$ axis. 

By taking measurements at much higher and much lower frequencies, we create a longer baseline for the graph. This makes the gradient (which represents the speed of sound $v$ since $\lambda = v \cdot \frac{1}{f}$) much more reliable and less sensitive to the $\pm 0.01 \text{ m}$ uncertainty in each wavelength reading.

highlights.text: over a wider range of frequencies

steps.title: Summary of improvements
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, here are the two distinct suggestions:

1. **Data Set & Range:** Collect more data points over a wider range of frequencies to improve the reliability of the best-fit line.
2. **Precision:** Use a more precise method for measuring wavelength (like an oscilloscope or computer analysis) to reduce the absolute uncertainty in each reading.

highlights.text: Collect more data points over a wider range of frequencies
Use a more precise method for measuring wavelength

steps.title: Analyze current uncertainty
steps.content: The question states that the current **uncertainty in wavelength** is $\pm 0.01 \text{ m}$. This likely comes from the limitations of manual measurement tools, such as a meter ruler. To improve the experiment, we need to focus on reducing this absolute uncertainty using higher-precision equipment.

steps.title: Use digital high-precision tools
steps.content:
One way to enhance measurement precision is to replace manual readings with digital ones. By using an **oscilloscope** connected to a microphone, the student can visualize the sound wave. 

Comparing the phase of the emitted signal with the received signal on the oscilloscope allows for a much more precise determination of the wavelength ($\lambda$) compared to using a ruler by eye. This directly reduces the "reading error" associated with identifying nodes or antinodes.

highlights.text: Use a more precise method for measuring wavelength, such as using an oscilloscope or computer-assisted sound analysis to reduce reading error.

steps.title: Increase data reliability
steps.content:
While the oscilloscope reduces the error of individual points, we can also improve the overall precision of the relationship by collecting more data. 

By taking readings over a **wider range of frequencies** and increasing the number of data points, the student can create a more reliable best-fit line. This reduces the uncertainty in the gradient of the graph, which represents the speed of sound ($v$) since $$v = f\lambda \implies \lambda = v\left(\frac{1}{f}\right)$$.

highlights.text: Collect more data points over a wider range of frequencies to improve the reliability of the best-fit line.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-76f9be9f-8db3-49cd-a2f3-26492bd0bfb1-1771929956947/652bb521-5f90-40b5-9e3c-baf64100c01a.jpeg)

At which position is a center of compression formed?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.2 Wave model > C.2.1 Classification of waves

options.markscheme:
- In a longitudinal wave, a compression occurs where particles are crowded together.
- For position B, particles to the left ($x < B$) have a positive displacement (displaced toward B).
- Particles to the right ($x > B$) have a negative displacement (displaced toward B).
- Therefore, particles converge at B, making it a center of compression.

- For position D, particles to the left ($x < D$) have a negative displacement (displaced away from D).
- Particles to the right ($x > D$) have a positive displacement (displaced away from D).
- This results in a rarefaction, not a compression.

questions.specification: A sound wave in air has a frequency of 500.0 Hz and a wavelength of 0.68 m.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.2 Wave model > C.2.2 Nature of wave propagation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2 Nature of wave propagation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the basic wave relationship v = fλ to calculate wave speed and then find new wavelength when frequency changes while speed stays constant (wave propagation parameters).

parts.content: Calculate the speed of sound in air.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula: $v = f\lambda$
- Substitute values: $v = 500.0 \times 0.68$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate speed: $v = 340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: To calculate the speed of sound, we first need to identify the variables provided in the problem. We are given the frequency and the wavelength of the sound wave.

steps.title: Select the wave equation
steps.content: In **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we learn that the speed of a wave is the product of its frequency and its wavelength. This relationship is found in the **Waves** section of your IB Physics Data Booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the equation to find the speed ($v$):

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f \lambda \\ v &= 500.0 \times 0.68 \\ v &= 340 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

In the exam, showing the substitution step is worth 1 mark (**M1**), and providing the correct final answer with units is worth the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Enter the multiplication in the Run-Matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication directly on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression in the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the speed by multiplying frequency and wavelength.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content: The speed of sound in air is $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Identify given wave parameters
steps.content:
To find the speed of sound using the period and wavelength, we first need to identify the values provided in the question. We are given the frequency ($f$) and the wavelength ($\lambda$).

- Frequency: $f = 500.0 \text{ Hz}$
- Wavelength: $\lambda = 0.68 \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate the wave period
steps.content:
The period ($T$) of a wave is the time it takes for one complete cycle to pass a fixed point. It is the reciprocal of the frequency:

$$T = \frac{1}{f}$$

Substituting our value:

$$T = \frac{1}{500.0} = 0.002 \text{ s}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 500.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the period of the wave.

steps.title: Determine the wave speed
steps.content:
Wave speed ($v$) can be defined as the distance a wave travels divided by the time it takes. For one complete cycle, the distance traveled is one wavelength ($\lambda$) and the time taken is one period ($T$):

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

Substituting our values:

$$v = \frac{0.68}{0.002} = 340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This approach demonstrates the fundamental relationship found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.68 divided by 0.002 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide the wavelength by the period to find the speed.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The speed of sound in air is calculated to be $340 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1. Showing the correct substitution into the formula (**M1**).
2. Providing the final answer with the correct units and significant figures (**A1**). Since the inputs were given to 4 and 2 significant figures, we typically match the least precise input, giving us 2 significant figures (340).

parts.content: If the frequency of the sound wave is doubled, what will be the new wavelength? Assume the speed of sound remains constant.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula: $\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$
- Substitute values: $\lambda = \frac{340}{1000.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate wavelength: $\lambda = 0.34 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the wave equation
steps.content:
To solve this using a two-step approach, we first need the relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength. This is found in the **Waves** section of your IB data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

steps.title: Calculate the speed of sound
steps.content:
Using the initial values provided in the question, we calculate the constant speed of sound ($v$). We are given a frequency of $500.0\text{ Hz}$ and a wavelength of $0.68\text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f \times \lambda \\ v &= 500.0 \times 0.68 \\ v &= 340\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the speed of sound by multiplying frequency and wavelength.

steps.title: Determine the new frequency
steps.content:
The question states that the frequency is doubled. We calculate the new frequency ($f_{new}$):

$$\begin{aligned} f_{new} &= 2 \times 500.0 \\ f_{new} &= 1000.0\text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: frequency of the sound wave is doubled

steps.title: Calculate the new wavelength
steps.content:
Since the speed of sound remains constant, we use our calculated speed ($340\text{ m s}^{-1}$) and the new frequency to find the new wavelength ($\lambda_{new}$). Rearranging the wave equation gives:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_{new} &= \frac{v}{f_{new}} \\ \lambda_{new} &= \frac{340}{1000.0} \\ \lambda_{new} &= 0.34\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

In the IB markscheme, you would earn one mark (**M1**) for correctly substituting the values into the rearranged formula and one mark (**A1**) for the final answer.

calculator.description: Calculate the new wavelength by dividing speed by the new frequency.

steps.title: Identify the wave relationship
steps.content:
To solve this using proportional reasoning, we start with the wave equation found in **Topic C.2 (Wave model)** of your syllabus and the **Waves** section of the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

In this problem, the question specifies that the speed of sound ($v$) remains constant. This means the product of frequency ($f$) and wavelength ($\lambda$) is a constant value.

steps.title: Determine the proportionality
steps.content:
Since $v$ is constant, we can rearrange the formula to see the relationship between wavelength and frequency:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

This shows that wavelength is **inversely proportional** to frequency ($\lambda \propto \frac{1}{f}$). If we increase the frequency, the wavelength must decrease by the same factor to keep the speed constant.

steps.title: Apply the scaling factor
steps.content:
The question states that the frequency is **doubled**. Using our inverse proportionality:

- If frequency is multiplied by $2$ ($f_{new} = 2f$),
- Then wavelength must be multiplied by $\frac{1}{2}$ ($\lambda_{new} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda$).

This direct comparison allows us to find the answer without necessarily calculating the speed of sound first.

highlights.text: frequency of the sound wave is doubled

steps.title: Calculate the final wavelength
steps.content:
Now, we apply this factor to the original wavelength of $0.68\text{ m}$:

$$\lambda_{new} = 0.68 \times \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\lambda_{new} = 0.34\text{ m}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the substitution (showing the relationship) and 1 mark for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: State what is meant by the principle of superposition.
parts.markscheme:
- The total displacement is the vector sum of individual displacements. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Context of wave interaction
steps.content: This question asks for a formal definition of how waves behave when they overlap. This concept is central to **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**, specifically regarding how multiple wave pulses interact to create interference patterns.

steps.title: State the verbal definition
steps.content: The principle of superposition states that when two or more waves meet at a point in space, the resultant (or total) displacement at that point is the vector sum of the individual displacements of the waves.

steps.title: Analyze the marking criteria
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your definition must include two specific components:

1.  **Total displacement**: You must refer to the combined effect as the "total" or "resultant" displacement.
2.  **Vector sum**: You must specify that the individual displacements are added as vectors. This is important because displacements can be positive or negative, allowing for both constructive and destructive interference.

$$y_{\text{total}} = y_1 + y_2 + ... + y_n$$

highlights.text: The total displacement is the vector sum of individual displacements.

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content: When two or more wave pulses meet at a point in a medium (like the rope mentioned in the question), they interact. The **principle of superposition** is the rule we use to determine the resulting shape of the wave during this interaction. This is a fundamental concept found in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Express the symbolic formula
steps.content:
The principle can be expressed using a simple mathematical relationship. If two waves meet at a specific point, the total effect is the sum of their individual effects:

$$y = y_1 + y_2$$

steps.title: Define the variables
steps.content:
To satisfy the IB requirements for this approach, we must explicitly define what each term in our formula represents:

- $y$ is the **resultant displacement** of the medium at that point.
- $y_1$ is the **displacement** that the first pulse would cause on its own.
- $y_2$ is the **displacement** that the second pulse would cause on its own.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining the formula and the definitions, the principle of superposition states that: 

**The total displacement is the vector sum of the individual displacements.**

In an IB exam, using the word "displacement" and "vector sum" (or simply "sum") is key to earning the mark.

parts.content: Describe what happens when the two pulses meet.
parts.markscheme:
- The pulses temporarily overlap / interfere / superpose. <marks>M1</marks>
- They combine to form a single resultant pulse. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content: When two wave pulses travel toward each other on a rope, we analyze their interaction using the **Principle of Superposition**. This principle states that when two or more waves meet, the total displacement at any point is the vector sum of the displacements of the individual waves.

steps.title: Temporal overlap
steps.content: As the pulses reach the same location, they will **temporarily overlap** or **interfere**. This is the moment of superposition where both wave disturbances are present in the same part of the rope at the same time.

steps.title: Forming a resultant pulse
steps.content:
During this overlap, the individual displacements of the pulses add together. This causes them to **combine to form a single resultant pulse**. 

If the pulses are on the same side of the rope (constructive interference), the resultant pulse will be larger. If they are on opposite sides (destructive interference), it will be smaller. Once they pass through each other, they continue on their paths as if they had never met.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks for this question, your description should include:
1. The fact that the pulses **temporarily overlap** or **superpose** (1 mark).
2. The fact that they **combine to form a single resultant pulse** (1 mark).

parts.content: State what occurs after the pulses have overlapped.
parts.markscheme:
- Each pulse continues unaffected with its original shape and speed. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understanding the Principle of Superposition
steps.content:
The **Principle of Superposition** is a fundamental concept in wave physics covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**. It states that when two or more waves meet, the resulting displacement is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements. 

A key property of this principle is that waves (including pulses on a rope) do not "collide" like solid objects; they simply pass through one another.

steps.title: Analyze the post-overlap state
steps.content:
Based on the nature of wave interference, once the two pulses have finished overlapping, they emerge from the interaction and continue traveling in their original directions. 

The Principle of Superposition implies that waves are temporary disturbances in the medium. Because they don't permanently alter the medium or each other, they pass through one another and emerge unchanged in:
*   **Shape** (their profile)
*   **Amplitude** (their height)
*   **Velocity** (their speed and direction)

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state that the pulses are unaffected by the encounter. 

After the overlap, each pulse continues with its **original shape and speed** as if the other pulse had never been there.

highlights.text: Each pulse continues unaffected with its original shape and speed.

parts.content: Define wavelength, frequency, and wave speed, and state the relationship between them.
parts.markscheme:
- **Wavelength**: The distance between two consecutive points in phase on a wave (e.g., two consecutive crests or troughs). <marks>A1</marks>
- **Frequency**: The number of oscillations or cycles per second, measured in hertz $(\mathrm{Hz})$. <marks>A1</marks>
- **Wave speed**: The distance traveled by a wave per unit of time. <marks>A1</marks>
- **Relationship**: $v = f \lambda$, where $v$ is the wave speed, $f$ is the frequency, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define Wavelength
steps.content:
First, we define **wavelength** (represented by the Greek letter lambda, $\lambda$). 

Wavelength is the shortest distance between two points that are in phase on a wave. This is most easily visualized as the distance between two consecutive **crests** or two consecutive **troughs**.

steps.title: Define Frequency
steps.content:
Next, we define **frequency** (represented by $f$).

Frequency is the number of complete oscillations (or cycles) that pass a given point per second. It is measured in **hertz** (Hz), where $1 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Define Wave Speed
steps.content:
Now, we define **wave speed** (represented by $v$).

Wave speed is the distance traveled by the wave profile per unit of time. It describes how fast the energy of the wave is moving through the medium.

steps.title: State the Relationship
steps.content:
Finally, we state the mathematical relationship connecting these three quantities. This is known as the **wave equation** and can be found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet.

$$v = f\lambda$$

Where:
- $v$ is the wave speed ($\text{m s}^{-1}$)
- $f$ is the frequency (Hz)
- $\lambda$ is the wavelength (m)

parts.content: A radio wave has a frequency of $95.0 \, \mathrm{MHz}$. Calculate its wavelength if the speed of light is $3.00 \times 10^8 \, \mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}$. State why radio waves are classified as electromagnetic waves.
parts.markscheme:
### Wavelength calculation:
- Use the relationship: $\lambda=\frac{v}{f}$
- Convert frequency: $f=95.0 \, \mathrm{MHz}=95.0 \times 10^6 \, \mathrm{Hz}$
- Substitute values: $\lambda=\frac{3.00 \times 10^8}{95.0 \times 10^6}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Final answer: $\lambda=3.16 \, \mathrm{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

### Why radio waves are electromagnetic:
- Radio waves are electromagnetic because they consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields and can propagate through a vacuum without requiring a medium. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Convert frequency to basic units
steps.content:
First, we must convert the given frequency from megahertz (MHz) to the standard SI unit of hertz (Hz). The prefix "M" (mega) stands for $10^6$.

$$f = 95.0 \text{ MHz} = 95.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$

steps.title: Select the wave equation
steps.content:
To find the wavelength, we use the fundamental relationship between wave speed, frequency, and wavelength found in the **Waves** section of the data booklet.

$$v=f\lambda$$

Since this is an electromagnetic wave, the speed $v$ is the speed of light, $c$. We rearrange the formula to solve for wavelength ($\lambda$):

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{c}{f}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values into the rearranged equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8}{95.0 \times 10^6}$$

Using a calculator:

ti84.instructions: Enter the division using scientific notation:

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
The calculation gives approximately $3.15789...$ meters. Since the given values ($95.0$ and $3.00$) have 3 significant figures, we round our answer to 3 significant figures.

$$\lambda = 3.16 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Explain electromagnetic classification
steps.content:
The second part asks why radio waves are classified as electromagnetic waves. This relates to the nature of their propagation and composition defined in **Topic C.2 Wave model**.

Radio waves are electromagnetic because:
1. They consist of **oscillating electric and magnetic fields** that are perpendicular to each other.
2. They do not require a material medium to travel, meaning they can **propagate through a vacuum**.
